It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Transnational private governance between the logics of empowerment and control Graeme Auld School of Public Policy and Administration, Carleton University, Ottawa, ON, Canada Stefan Renckens Department of Political Science, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada Benjamin Cashore School of Forestry and Environmental Studies, Yale University, New Haven, CT, USA Abstract Transnational private governance initiatives that address problems of social and environmental concern now pervade many sectors. In tackling distinct substantive problems, these programs have, however, prioritized different problem-oriented logics in their institutionalized rules and procedures. One is a “logic of control” that focuses on ameliorating environmental and social externalities by establishing strict and enforceable rules; another is a“logic of empowerment”that concentrates on remedying the exclusion of marginalized actors in the global economy. Examining certiﬁcation programs in the areas of fair trade, organic agriculture, ﬁsheries, and forest management, we assess the evolutionary effects of programs prioritizing one logic and then having to accommodate the other. The challenges programs face when balancing between the two logics, we argue, elucidate speciﬁc distributional consequences for wealth, power, and regulatory capabilities that private governance programs seek to overcome. Keywords: certiﬁcation, distributional impacts, environmental governance, private governance, social governance. 1. Introduction Transnational private governance initiatives (hereafter private governance) that address environmental and social concerns are now prominent across economic sectors. They encourage corporations and producers to adopt responsible business practices, and they often include certiﬁcation and product labels for signaling such improved business behavior to access potential market advantage. From resource management to industrial production processes (Bartley 2003), private governance has been championed to address critical challenges, including ﬁsheries depletion (Gulbrandsen 2010), forest degradation (Meidinger 2006), climate change (Levin et al. 2009), biofuel and agricultural product sustainability (Partzsch 2011), and electronic waste recycling (Renckens 2015). These initiatives share a common concern for the social domain. However, the diversity of problems of the social domain has engendered distinct problem-oriented logics (i.e. explanations for how institutions will solve problems) that shape actors’ideas about how private governance initiatives ought to be organized. Far from converging towards a dominant institutional logic in an emerging ﬁeld (Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009), we ﬁnd that competing, problem- oriented logics affect the evolving rules and practices of private governance, a pattern consistent with recent work on institutional and organizational change (e.g. Schneiberg & Soule 2005; Lounsbury 2007). From the many possible logics affecting private governance, we focus on two. Each, we show, has inﬂuenced the emergence and design of private certiﬁcation institutions, and which, once institutionalized, has then shaped and constrained agent choices surrounding speciﬁc policy content. First, a “logic of control” emphasizes ameliorating Correspondence: Graeme Auld, School of Public Policy and Administration, Carleton University, Ottawa, ON K1S5B6, Canada. It advances the idea that certain beneﬁcial activities (e.g. improved environmental performance) are generally costly to undertake and, hence, require institutions capable of controlling behavior to ensure compliance. This logic encompasses a belief that without prescriptive rules, compliance audits, and incentives for compliance, operators would be enticed to join certiﬁcation programs and then do nothing to change their behavior – leading to “green washing” and/or ineffectiveness. Second, a “logic of empowerment” captures ideas about the exclusion of marginalized actors in the global economy. It advances prescriptions for how institutions can improve the socio-economic situation of the direct targets of private governance, for example, small farmers from the global South. The logic of empowerment then holds that programs ought to help economically excluded and marginalized participants by giving them advice, ﬁnding ways to enhance their market position, collaborating to improve management, and advocating for their interests on the global stage. While ideas about stakeholder participation as a means for empowerment relate to our second logic, we argue that they are important to analyze separately. Indeed, we show how a focus on such stakeholder participation has been adopted more pervasively as a means for seeking inclusion within private governance, both in programs that started from a logic of control and from a logic of empowerment. This underscores a key point: the logics encompass different ideas for how to solve problems within the social domain. This is important as we ﬁnd that the logic a program prioritizes in its initial design affects how that program evolves: programs that began by prioritizing empowerment have faced increasing demands to institutionalize the logic of control, and vice versa. Hence, in ways important for scholars and practitioners to understand, both the initial logic prioritized (i.e. ideas) and the institutionalized rules the logic supports (i.e. a program’s rules and procedures) shape the way program agents address speciﬁc policy choices as the program evolves. In this respect, our analysis identiﬁes the consequences of overlapping ﬁelds (Fligstein & McAdam 2012, pp. 9–13)1 where different ideas about the role of private governance intermix and are contested. As separate ﬁelds of private governance across issue areas and sectors have begun to merge (compare Bartley 2003; Bartley & Smith 2010), we observe the challenges programs have faced when balancing between the two. By discussing the means by which private governance has attempted to overcome these challenges, we elucidate their distributional consequences in terms of wealth (i.e. the distribution of aggregate, accumulated wealth), power (i.e. the distribution of who exercises control over others), and regulatory capabilities (i.e. the distribution of the potential to exert regulatory power), as speciﬁed by Cafaggi and Pistor (2012; 2015). It lies beyond the scope of this article to propose an explanation for why one or another logic informed the design of any given program. Our purpose is simply to identify these distinct logics and reveal why they are important. We therefore encourage future research to explore the mechanisms by which programs experience pressure to accom- modate one or the other logic. Many plausible mechanisms exist, including: changes in the macro-political mood raising the salience of the problem one logic is expected to address (e.g. the growth hormone and Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy [BSE] crises in Europe that helped justify more stringent EU risk regulation following our logic of control, see Vogel 2012, pp. 54–66; Mattli & Büthe 2005); learning processes, where information is exchanged among program ofﬁcials and stakeholders to create new understandings of how problems might be solved by following an alternative logic (e.g. the emulation of the Fairtrade model by the Forest Stewardship Council [FSC], see Auld 2014a); or political contestation, where different participants in a program’s policy community seek to transform rules to better ﬁt the logic serving their organizational interests (Mattli & Büthe 2005; Auld 2014b). To be clear, we are not arguing that the competing logics and the paths programs take to accommodate them are the only factors that determine the distributional outcomes of private governance (see, Mattli & Büthe 2005). Indeed, we document that exogenous political-economic conditions and the characteristics of the sectors in which private governance develops interact with program rules to inﬂuence whether collective action problems or econo- mies of scale preclude some actors from beneﬁting from private governance. Rather, our focus, consistent with Lall’s (2014) analysis of how ﬁrst-movers shape the winners and losers from ﬁnancial and accounting standards, is to show that examining the ideas our logics advance can both elucidate speciﬁc distributional consequences, and, as impor- tantly, that these consequences can then change over time as programs are pressed to accommodate competing logics. The analysis builds from 15 years of research examining private governance. We draw on stakeholder interviews, a review of primary documents, and a synthesis of ﬁndings from the research of others. Though the logics were largely derived inductively, the analysis draws insights from neo- institutionalism and work on participatory and empowerment evaluation to theoretically ground our observations. The article proceeds in three parts. First, we deﬁne transnational private governance and outline its key features. Second, we describe the logics of control and empowerment. Third, we highlight two separate paths programs can travel – one from an emphasis on control to attention for empowerment and vice versa – while discussing both common and unique challenges to these paths and their distributional consequences. 2. Transnational private governance Transnational private governance has emerged in the last two decades as both an alternative and complement to public regulation (Bartley 2003; Levin et al. 2009). One of the main characteristics of private, or non-state, gover- nance is that the state is not directly involved in standard setting. It is private actors (e.g. non-governmental organizations [NGOs], corporations, or business associations) that set the rules. While hybrid conﬁgurations exist wherein the state helps set the rules – such as recent roundtable standard setting models for biofuels or water governance – in this article we focus on purely private governance arrangements (cf. Abbott & Snidal 2009). In these cases, the state does not – at least initially – require adherence to the privately developed rules. States, however, do play an important supporting role, as private rules are not developed in a vacuum; they emerge within a larger public regulatory and institutional context, including property and contract law. States can also be more directly involved through ﬁnancial or in-kind support of private governance, through public procurement policies requiring certiﬁed products, or by having state-owned enterprises certiﬁed (Cashore 2002). A private standard setting initiative’s “transnational” character then derives from its functioning not being conﬁned to the boundaries of one state’s territory, but rather to supply chains that operate across borders. A further distinction involves the role of the market. One category, termed non-market private standards, is deﬁned as “rule-making by an international nongovernmental organization that is viewed by public and private actors alike as the obvious forum for the issue in question; in other words, it is the focal institution” (Büthe & Mattli 2011, p. 29). The International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) and the International Organization for Stan- dardization (ISO) are two such organizations; for various reasons (e.g. formal recognition within trade agreements and market efﬁciencies from product harmonization), their standard setting power across a range of technical product standards is widely accepted. Distributional conﬂicts over which standard should be adopted by ISO and IEC do occur (Büthe & Mattli 2011); however, once adopted, compliance is often beneﬁcial for actors. By contrast, market-based initiatives – which are the focus of this article – depend on market demand and company support for their survival and authority. This implies that competition can exist between various standard setting initiatives that employ different standards for similar products or production processes (Büthe & Mattli 2011, pp. 25–29). Market-based initiatives are classiﬁed in various ways. Abbott and Snidal (2009) focus on the actors involved in standard setting and the stages of a regulatory governance process. Other analysts use the character of compliance incentives, enforcement mechanisms, and stakeholder involvement (Fransen & Kolk 2007; Fransen 2011). These characteristics help distinguish corporate or sectoral codes of conduct and environmental management systems from stricter third-party audited certiﬁcation programs. Similarly, Potoski and Prakash (2009) focus on the strict- ness of the standard and enforcement mechanisms to differentiate between programs. The initiatives we examine have evolved to become “complete” regimes (see Scott 2002); they include processes for rulemaking, implementation, compliance and enforcement, and sometimes adjudication (Auld 2014b). Although others have disaggregated private rulemakers to understand the actors and stakes involved in “stages” of a regulatory governance sequence (Büthe 2010, p. 315), we focus on how programs deﬁne the issue they develop standards for and the resulting consequences for governance design as a whole. This is important because different components of private governance, as complete regimes, can serve different functions depending on the logic at play. Hence, we use the term private governance, and not private regulation, because seeing standards, enforcement, and compliance through a regulatory lens already implies the ideas of control. Distinguishing between the logics of empowerment and control, we argue, helps to unpack the conﬂation of private governance and regulation for market-based initiatives addressing issues in the social domain. Logics of control and empowerment The problems addressed by private governance provide distinct normative underpinnings. We examine two such problems: overcoming marginalization of peripheral actors in the global economy; and ameliorating environmental and social harm resulting from capitalist industrialization and globalization. These normative ends justify programs being based on different logics. We deﬁne logics as explanations for how an institution is expected to solve an inter-subjectively deﬁned problem. Narrower and more speciﬁc than institutional logics (Thornton et al. 2012), they are closest to instrument logics (i.e. the general norms that guide the approach taken to implementing policy goals, Howlett & Cashore 2009) and programmatic ideas (i.e.“elite prescriptions that enable politicians, corporate leaders, and other decision makers to chart a clear and speciﬁc course of action,” Campbell 2004, p. 94). They embody a causal theory of how procedural and substantive rules will lead to certain outcomes. Put differently, taking insights from practice and theory, logics prescribe a course of action to advance some end. The logics we examine focus on how private governance, through an initial problem deﬁnition, seeks to inﬂuence its “targets” – the producers or operators at the primary stage of production in a given supply chain. We do not claim to have identiﬁed the only logics relevant to private governance,3 and the logics do not reﬂect all of the problems programs were pushed to address at their outsets. Institutionalization often narrows and simpliﬁes issues akin to how the diverse grievances of a social movement can be narrowed by organizations that emerge from a movement (e.g. NGOs), or via policy responses adopted by targeted governments or corporations.We are interested in the logics of these institutionalized responses as, we suggest, the two logics identiﬁed have distributional implications for the activities and evolution of private governance, and that these issues are underexplored. The logic of control is underpinned by neo-institutional theories that highlight problems of opportunism, free riding, and achieving collective action (Olson 1971; Williamson 1985; North 1990).4 This logic asserts that there are certain beneﬁcial activities (e.g. improving corporate environmental and social performance) that are costly to undertake. Without rules to ensure these activities actually occur, compliance will be minimal. Control in this case encompasses several components: prescriptive rules, independent certiﬁcation, accreditation, and product tracking. A focus on prescriptive rules emanates from a concern that without clear, non-discretionary requirements, operators that participate in private governance will not meet sustainability objectives. Independent certiﬁcation is then performed to ensure that participants actually adhere to the rules and minimize shirking. There indeed exists an economic incentive for non-compliance: if all participants comply with the standard, it is beneﬁcial for an individual company to avoid compliance costs while not jeopardizing business opportunities (cf. the Prisoners’ dilemma and cooperation problems) (Abbott & Snidal 2009; Potoski & Prakash 2009). Accreditation offers further control. Programs have created or contracted to external organizations to perform as arm’s length certiﬁers to assess applicants in accordance with the private governance standards. Tracking products along supply chains and percentage-base claims (i.e. rules for how much certiﬁed content a ﬁnal product must contain to carry a program’s label) are a ﬁnal control mechanism to advance credible claims. The logic of empowerment, by contrast, identiﬁes vast differences in the opportunities available to economic actors. Following longstanding concerns about the declining terms-of-trade for primary products in global trade (Bloch & Sapsford 2000), as well as about alternative, counter-hegemonic production practices, it is informed by an explicit aim to redistribute power, control, and resources to marginalized actors in global supply chains. Its solution is informed by participatory research and empowerment and participatory evaluation, which question the value of objective assessment by external actors as appropriate for advancing development (Rappaport 1987; Chambers 1994).5 It sees evaluation as an end in itself; a process whereby participation of those affected by the program creates knowledge, empowerment, and, ultimately, improvements in their situations (Korten 1980; Fetterman 1994; Zimmerman 1995; Jackson & Kassam 1998). Equally, it questions the need for and ability of external interventions that impose top-down ideas of how development or resource management ought to occur (Korten 1980; Fetterman 1994); it advocates rules and procedures for monitoring that are designed by and/or accountable to those that are involved in resource extraction (Ostrom 1990). Hence, rather than emphasizing formal institutions to check and verify compliance with pre-established prescriptive rules, this logic envisions a networked and relational approach to addressing problems in the production and distribution of goods and services. It is about giving greater power and resources to the “targets” of private governance to facilitate self-determination. The second concerns participation. This focuses on inclusion in decisionmaking processes, while not explicitly addressing redistribution or decentralized power to a program’s “targets.”We make this distinction clear because it may lead to confusion over how we have classiﬁed certiﬁcation programs; indeed, empowerment through inclusion is more pervasive across certiﬁcation programs, whereas the redistributive and power decentralization variant – which we focus on here – is more rare. Identifying these logics is important as they reﬂect the priorities institutionalized in the early designs of private governance programs (Fig. 1). Programs tackling problems of the public domain confront institutional-design choices. It is the logic of these institutional designs that we must assess to understand the distributional conse- quences of private governance (see Lall 2014). Moreover, work tracing the history of certiﬁcation programs has noted the increasing interactions among programs (Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009; Bartley & Smith 2010; Green 2013), while also clarifying their roots in different communities that often held distinct ideas of how best to address problems in the social domain (Bartley 2003). Figure 1 conceptualizes these empirical ﬁndings as they affect the relationship between the two logics. Typically, programs began by following one of the two logics more explicitly; they have since had to adapt to accommodate demands that the other logic also be addressed. These programs can, thus, be seen as following two evolutionary paths: an “empower then control” path (pathway 1), and a “control then empower” path (pathway 2). It is beyond the scope of this article to trace the origins of the logics.6 The analysis that follows instead starts from the empirical observation that private governance has evolved to face pressure to accommodate both the logics of control and empowerment. In turn, we argue, that each of the logics, and their subsequent intermixing, have distinct distributional implications. Evolutionary paths and distributional implications There are both distinct and common challenges programs face along the two paths. This section reviews these challenges, focusing on their distributional implications for wealth, power, and regulatory capability, and how programs have sought to overcome them. 4.1. Pathway one: Control then empower For programs that follow the “control then empower” path, the initial concern is developing institutions to ensure credible and rigorous claims to distinguish themselves from other types of corporate social responsibility (CSR) initiatives that lack enforceable rules for participants (Auld et al. 2008). When these programs also seek legitimacy by focusing on empowerment, they confront the challenge of not undermining established mechanisms for control. On this “control then empower” path, the self-selection problem is key: only those companies and/or operators that practice at a relatively high level tend to participate because of the costs associated with meeting the high standards of control, both for auditing and adjusting actual practices. Hence, most efforts to make these programs’ systems of control accessible for various marginalized actors focus on challenges relating to producer’s size, geographic loca- tion, and limited expertise or knowledge. Experiences of the forest and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation ﬁelds capture these dynamics well. 4.1.1. Size discrimination – enhancing participation of small operators Research notes that the product-tracking rules that follow the logic of control favor large and vertically integrated ﬁrms. Tracking requirements for forest certiﬁcation have meant that companies with processing facilities and forestlands have been better positioned to certify (Cashore et al. 2004). A related difﬁculty is associated with economies of scale. The reduced unit costs of audits that result from large ownership create potentially huge disadvantages for small operators (Haener & Luckert 1998; Ponte 2008; Ebeling & Yasué 2009). Size discrimination is also a problem because the buyers seeking to source certiﬁed products often want large volumes, not the small quantities available from smaller operators (Klooster 2005). Several programs have sought to overcome these facets of size discrimination. The FSC learned from the Rainforest Alliance’s Smartwood program to quickly introduce various mechanisms to address problems of small owners. These include procedures for group certiﬁcation (i.e. where separate landowners are certiﬁed together to reduce the per-owner costs of being audited) and resource manager certiﬁcation (i.e. where a consultant is certiﬁed for the management services he or she provides to forest owners) (Morris & Dunne 2004). The Marine Stewardship Council (MSC) has also developed an approach to assess small-scale, data-deﬁcient ﬁsheries that would otherwise be ineligible because of the program’s requirements for scientiﬁc data on a ﬁshery’s track record and current status. In 2007, the MSC began a pilot project to provide these ﬁsheries better access to the program (Marine Stewardship Council 2007b), and while some ﬁsheries have enrolled, this remains a problem. Opening up programs to smaller producers, thus, intends to overcome exclusion resulting from the original program design, while less intensive auditing, fewer data requirements, and cost dissemination are ways to empower more marginalized producers and adhere to principles of fairness and equal access, while, nonetheless, continuing to prioritize control and consistency. 4.1.2. Spatial discrimination – enhancing participation of operators in developing countries Although within countries there is considerable variation in the capacities of operators to participate in private governance, existing trends in uptake highlight that operators from developing countries face greater barriers to participation. Several authors refer to the high direct costs of auditing and certiﬁcation as the main impediments to greater uptake in the global South (Thornber et al. 1999; Morris & Dunne 2004; Vandergeest 2007; Ponte 2008; Ebeling & Yasué 2009). Veriﬁcation or auditing of compliance can be expensive, as it involves ﬁeld visits by experts, signiﬁcant paper work, and follow-up to ensure practices continue. But there are other challenges too. First, there are policy issues. Those operators that must perform to a certain level to adhere to domestic law will ﬁnd participating in certiﬁcation, all else equal, easier than operators practicing in areas where laws are weak, or exist but are not enforced. Furthermore, the presence of stronger institutions (e.g. relating to land tenure or ﬁshing quota security) also increases the potential of certiﬁcation. For instance, Ebeling and Yasué (2009) compared forest certiﬁcation in Bolivia and Ecuador to ﬁnd that stronger government enforcement of forestry regulations, Logics of empowerment and control G. Auld et al. © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 113 Page 7: compatibility of domestic forestry laws with certiﬁcation requirements, and tax beneﬁts to certiﬁed producers increase the likelihood of companies seeking certiﬁcation. Second, there are barriers associated with limited technical knowledge about product qualities, limited connec- tion to market channels (particularly accessing international markets), limited marketing expertise, and poor infrastructure. Pattberg (2006), for example, provides two reasons why certifying tropical forests is more expensive than temperate or boreal forests: tropical forests are more complex and we have limited technical knowledge of how to manage them. In both instances, compliance costs increase as certiﬁcation requires more preparation and time. Furthermore, poor infrastructure can inhibit smooth tracking of certiﬁed products and impose extra costs (Cashore et al. 2006). Costs again rise when companies need to establish relationships with new suppliers that can meet private governance standards (Morris & Dunne 2004). Finally, private governance programs may also be less well resourced to certify in these parts of the world. A lack of accredited auditors in the global South can lead to costly delays in the certiﬁcation process, whereas competition between multiple auditors is expected to reduce costs (Morris & Dunne 2004; Ponte 2008). A key concern with the limited uptake in the global South is that certiﬁcation might become a non-tariff trade barrier (Pattberg 2006; Delzeit & Holm-Müller 2009). If retailers in the North pledge to sell mainly or only certiﬁed products and these products are sourced relatively less from countries of the global South, this may prevent poorer producers from accessing these markets. Certiﬁcation might, thus, make it harder for producers in certain countries to access global markets (Fuchs et al. 2009). Private governance systems have adopted several strategies to overcome the challenges operators in these countries face. Many programs have procedures for tailoring global principles and criteria to the social, political, and ecological conditions of a region. The FSC does this by delegating standards setting work to national initiatives or, when these standards are not yet in place, letting accredited auditors develop generic standards for a region. Similarly, the MSC gives the auditor control over this localization process (Gulbrandsen 2010). The MSC also launched its Developing World Program, an initiative designed to get more stakeholders from developing countries involved in and aware of the MSC, and ensure the MSC standard remains relevant to these ﬁsheries (Marine Stewardship Council 2007a, 2008). Outside the programs there have been additional attempts by foundations and development assistance agencies to provide funds to increase participation in certain countries (Klooster 2006). 4.1.3. The imposition of strict and prescriptive rules, both regarding substantive aspects of production processes and auditing, generates a clear distinction between regulators and regulatory targets, as those in charge of making the rules have larger regulatory capability and exert power over those that must follow the rules.
